expectations.text: Uses an interface or abstract type for notification channels (OCP/DIP)
expectations.evidence: NotificationChannel.java defines a public interface with send(Notification) and channelName() methods. All three channel implementations (EmailNotificationChannel, SmsNotificationChannel, PushNotificationChannel) implement this interface. NotificationService depends only on the NotificationChannel interface, not concrete classes.

expectations.text: Each channel (email, SMS, push) is a separate class implementing the interface (SRP)
expectations.evidence: Three separate classes exist: EmailNotificationChannel implements NotificationChannel, SmsNotificationChannel implements NotificationChannel, PushNotificationChannel implements NotificationChannel. Each is in its own file and handles only its specific delivery mechanism with its own config object.

expectations.text: NotificationService accepts channels via constructor injection, not internal instantiation (DIP)
expectations.evidence: NotificationService has a private constructor that takes List<NotificationChannel>. Channels are provided externally via static factory methods: NotificationService.of(NotificationChannel... channels) and NotificationService.fromChannels(List<NotificationChannel>). No channel is ever instantiated inside NotificationService.

expectations.text: Failure in one channel does not prevent other channels from sending (graceful degradation)
expectations.evidence: In NotificationService.send(), each channel is called in a for loop with individual try/catch blocks (lines 103-109): 'try { channel.send(notification); succeeded.add(channel.channelName()); } catch (NotificationException e) { failures.add(...); }'. A caught exception adds to failures list but the loop continues to the next channel.

expectations.text: Configuration objects are immutable or use a builder pattern (Effective Java Items 2/17)
expectations.evidence: All three config classes (SmtpConfig, SmsConfig, PushConfig) are declared 'final', have private constructors taking a Builder, all fields are private final, and expose only accessor methods. Each provides a static builder() factory method returning a nested Builder class. For example, SmtpConfig.builder("host", "from").port(587).useTls(true).credentials("user", "pass").build(). Notification also uses builder pattern with immutable fields.

expectations.text: No raw types in generic code — proper use of parameterized types (Item 26)
expectations.evidence: All generic usages are properly parameterized: List<NotificationChannel> in NotificationService, List<String> and List<ChannelFailure> in NotificationResult, List<NotificationChannel> in the of() factory method. No raw List, Map, or other generic types appear anywhere in the codebase.

expectations.text: Returns a result object or collection rather than void/boolean to report per-channel status
expectations.evidence: NotificationService.send() returns NotificationResult, which contains List<String> succeededChannels and List<ChannelFailure> failures. It provides isFullSuccess(), isPartialSuccess(), and isCompleteFailure() query methods. ChannelFailure is a record holding channelName and the NotificationException.

expectations.text: Uses static factory methods or builders for object creation where appropriate (Items 1-2)
expectations.evidence: Static factory methods are used extensively: NotificationService.of(...) and NotificationService.fromChannels(...), EmailNotificationChannel.of(config), SmsNotificationChannel.of(config), PushNotificationChannel.of(config), Notification.builder(...), SmtpConfig.builder(...), SmsConfig.builder(...), PushConfig.builder(...). NotificationService's constructor is private, enforcing use of factories.

claims.evidence: Fields are private final, lists use List.copyOf() in constructor which creates unmodifiable copies, class is declared final, no mutating methods exist.
Class is declared final, all fields are private final, private constructor, no setters. Uses builder pattern for construction.

claims.claim: Channel implementations can be tested by overriding doSend()
claims.evidence: The channel classes are declared 'final' (e.g., 'public final class EmailNotificationChannel'), which means doSend() cannot actually be overridden via subclassing despite being declared 'protected'. This is contradictory. Testing would require a different approach (e.g., dependency injection of a transport layer or using the NotificationChannel interface directly).

suggestions.reason: No assertion checks whether the code compiles. All assertions can be satisfied by syntactically plausible but non-compilable code. Consider adding a compilation check assertion.

No assertion checks for unit tests. A production-quality implementation would include tests demonstrating graceful degradation and per-channel result reporting. Consider adding 'Includes at least one test class that exercises multi-channel failure scenarios'.

suggestions.assertion: Failure in one channel does not prevent other channels from sending (graceful degradation)
suggestions.reason: This assertion is verified by code inspection of the try/catch structure, but a stronger version would require a test that actually demonstrates this behavior by configuring a failing channel alongside a succeeding one.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions cover the key design principles well (SOLID, Effective Java patterns). The main gap is the absence of any compilation or runtime verification -- all assertions are satisfied by static code inspection alone.
